analogy: Think of "Water" through the chapter's own opening images: 5.1: Water Cycle Condensation Precipitation Run off Evaporation 5 Water When you think of water, what images come to your mind? You think of rivers, the waterfalls, the pitter patter of raindrops, water in your taps... Children love to float paper boats in rain puddles.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 5 Water, WATER 31, 32 OUR ENVIRONMENT, 1 Drop = rivers. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Water is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 5.1: Water Cycle Condensation Precipitation Run off Evaporation 5 Water When you think of water, what images come to your mind?

The text explains that you think of rivers, the waterfalls, the pitter patter of raindrops, water in your taps...

The text explains that children love to float paper boats in rain puddles.

The text explains that the sun’s heat causes evaporation of water into vapour.

The text explains that when the water vapour cools down, it condenses and forms clouds.

The text explains that from there it may fall on the land or sea in the form of rain, snow or sleet.

The text explains that the process by which water continually changes its form and circulates between oceans, atmosphere and land is known as the water cycle (Fig 5.1).

The text explains that the same water that existed centuries ago still exists today.

The text explains that the water used to irrigate a field in Haryana may have flowed down the Amazon River a hundred years ago.

The text explains that the major sources of fresh water are the rivers, ponds, springs and glaciers.

The text explains that the ocean bodies and the seas contain salty water.

The text explains that the water of the oceans is salty or saline as it contains large Terrarium: It is an artificial enclosure for keeping small house plants.

The text explains that plant the largest plants first and then arrange the smaller ones around them.

The text explains that spray the arrangement with water and close the jar.

The text explains that the water that evaporates from the leaves and soil condenses and falls back in the form of drops of water.

The text explains that 5.2: World – Major Seas, Lakes and Rivers WATER 31 amount of dissolved salts.

The text explains that let it represent the total water on the surface of the earth.

The text explains that the 12 spoons of water that was taken in a bowl is the total amount of fresh water on earth.

The text explains that dISTRIBUTION OF WATER BODIES We all know that three-fourth of the earth surface is covered by water.

The text explains that if there is more water than land on this earth, why do so many countries face water scarcity?

The text explains that now don’t you think we are wasting a precious resource when we use water carelessly?

The text explains that the wet sand on the beach, the cool breeze, the seabirds, the smell of the salt in the air and music of the waves; everything is so fascinating.

The text explains that the movements that occur in oceans can be broadly categorised as: waves, tides and currents.

The text explains that 34 OUR ENVIRONMENT Waves When you are playing throw ball on the beach and the ball falls into the water, what happens?

The text explains that it is fun to watch how the ball gets washed back to the shore by the waves.

The text explains that when the water on the surface of the ocean rises and falls alternately, they are called waves.

The text explains that tsunami is a Japanese word that means “Harbour waves” as the harbours get destroyed whenever there is tsunami.

The text explains that tSUNAMI – THE EARTH’S PANDEMONIUM Tsunami or the harbour wave struck havoc in the Indian Ocean on the 26 December 2004.

The text explains that the wave was the result of the earthquake that had its epicenter close to the western boundary of Sumatra.

The text explains that the magnitude of the earthquake was 9.0 on the Richter scale.

The text explains that as the Indian plate went under the Burma plate, there was a sudden movement of the sea floor, causing the earthquake.

The text explains that the ocean floor was displaced by about 10 – 20m and tilted in a downwardly direction.

The text explains that waves are formed when winds scrape across the ocean surface.

The text explains that the stronger the wind blows, the bigger the wave becomes.

The text explains that 5.4: Waves During a storm, the winds blowing at very high speed form huge waves.

The text explains that an earthquake, a volcanic eruption or underwater landslides can shift large amounts of ocean water.

The text explains that as a result a huge tidal wave called tsunami, that may be as high as 15m. is formed.

The text explains that these waves travel at a speed of more than 700 km. per hour.

The text explains that as the wave moved from earthquake epicenter from Sumatra towards the Andaman islands and Sri Lanka the wave length decreased with decreasing depth of water.

The text explains that tsunami waves travelled upto a depth of 3 km. from the coast killing more than 10,000 people and affected more than lakh of houses.

The text explains that while the earthquake cannot be predicted in advance, it is possible to give a three-hour notice of a potential tsunami.

The text explains that such early warning systems are in place across the Pacific ocean, but not in the Indian Ocean.

The text explains that the large damage caused to life and property was primarily a result of lack of monitoring, the early warning systems and knowledge among the coast dwellers of Indian ocean.

The text explains that the first indication that tsunami is approaching is the rapid withdrawal of water from the coastal region, followed by destructive wave.

The text explains that as a consequence there was a large casualty of curious onlookers when the gigantic wave (tsunami) struck.

The text explains that 36 OUR ENVIRONMENT The strong gravitational pull exerted by the sun and the moon on the earth’s surface causes the tides.

The text explains that the water of the earth closer to the moon gets pulled under the influence of the moon’s gravitational force and causes high tide.

The text explains that during the full moon and new moon days, the sun, the moon and the earth are in the same line and the tides are highest.

The text explains that but when the moon is in its first and last quarter, the ocean waters get drawn in diagonally opposite directions by the gravitational pull of sun and moon resulting in low tides.

The text explains that oCEAN CURRENTS Ocean currents are streams of water flowing constantly on the ocean surface in definite directions.

The text explains that (Hint: A distributary on the Ganga-Brahmputra delta) (c) Either jealousy or anger cause a person’s fall (Hint: Name of a juicy fruit!) (d) Bhavani germinated the seeds in a pot (Hint: Look for her in West Africa) (e) “I am a zonal champion now” declared the excited athlete.

The text explains that (Hint: The river that has the biggest basin in the world) (f) The tiffin box rolled down and all the food fell in dusty potholes.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Environment, Chapter 5: Water
